steps.title: Recognize the essential identification
steps.content: ”Thymol and methane” appears immediately after “compounds” to identify which specific compounds are meant. This is essential identifying information—it tells us precisely which substances are being discussed.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule
steps.content:
When a phrase is essential to identify which noun you mean, it uses no commas; when it's optional extra information about something already identified, commas appear before and after.

Since “thymol and methane” directly specifies which compounds are being discussed, no commas are needed around it.

The comma-using alternatives incorrectly add commas, and placing a comma between the subject and verb is also incorrect.

That is the correct answer.
